import pandas as pd

# Visualization

# Preprocessing

from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

    roc_auc_score, average_precision_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report
)

from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline as ImbPipeline

2. Data Quality Issues and Preprocessing¶

2.1 Invalid and Anomalous Values¶

In [74]:
data['tenure'].unique()
Out[74]:
array([ 1., 34.,  2., 45.,  8., 22., 10., 28., 62., 13., 16., 58., 49.,
       25., 69., 52., 71., 21., 12., 30., 72., 17., 27.,  5., 46., 11.,
       70., 63., 43., 15., 60., 18., 66., 47.,  9.,  3., 31., 50., 64.,
       56.,  7., 42., 35., 48., 29., 65., 38., 68., 32., 55., 37., 36.,
       41.,  6.,  4., 33., 67., 23., 57., 61., 14., 20., 53., 40., 59.,
       24., 44., 19., 54., 51., 26.,  0., 39., 73., 74.])

Negative tenure values are not logically possible and likely result from data entry or system errors. These values were treated as invalid and set to missing (NaN) rather than converted to absolute values to avoid artificial distortion.

In [7]:
data.loc[data['tenure'] < 0, 'tenure'] = np.nan

2.2 Cleaning Numeric Columns¶

In [8]:
#Clean MonthlyCharges
data['MonthlyCharges'] = data['MonthlyCharges'].astype(str).str.replace(r'[^0-9.]', '', regex=True)
data['MonthlyCharges'] = data['MonthlyCharges'].replace('', np.nan)
data['MonthlyCharges'] = data['MonthlyCharges'].astype(float)

#2. Clean TotalCharges
data['TotalCharges'] = data['TotalCharges'].astype(str).str.replace(r'[^0-9.]', '', regex=True)
data['TotalCharges'] = data['TotalCharges'].replace('', np.nan)
data['TotalCharges'] = data['TotalCharges'].astype(float)

Tenure¶

Tenure is a critical predictor of churn. Since imputing tenure could introduce bias and harm model performance, rows with missing tenure values were removed.

In [10]:
data = data.dropna(subset=['tenure'])

MonthlyCharges¶

In [11]:
data['MonthlyCharges'].isna().sum()
Out[11]:
np.int64(287)

MonthlyCharges is determined primarily by service configuration, including internet type, contract length, and value-added services. Customers with similar service profiles are expected to have comparable monthly charges. So we will impute monthly charges based on similar customer profiles.

In [12]:
group_cols = [
    'InternetService',
    'Contract',
    'OnlineSecurity',
    'TechSupport',
    'StreamingTV',
    'StreamingMovies',
    'PhoneService'
]

monthly_charge_median = (
    data
    .groupby(group_cols)['MonthlyCharges']
    .median()
)

def impute_monthly_charges(row):
    if pd.isna(row['MonthlyCharges']):
        try:
            return monthly_charge_median.loc[
                tuple(row[col] for col in group_cols)
            ]
        except KeyError:
            return np.nan
    return row['MonthlyCharges']

data['MonthlyCharges'] = data.apply(impute_monthly_charges, axis=1)
In [13]:
data['MonthlyCharges'].isna().sum()
Out[13]:
np.int64(0)

TotalCharges¶

Due to real-world billing complexities such as prorated charges and one-time fees, TotalCharges was not imputed or back-calculated. It was retained for EDA but excluded from predictive modeling to prevent noise, multicollinearity, and data leakage.

In [14]:
data['TotalCharges'].isna().sum()
Out[14]:
np.int64(1128)

Methodology: Missing Value Treatment

  • Invalid tenure values were treated as missing and removed due to high business importance.
  • MonthlyCharges was imputed using median values of customers with similar service profiles.
  • TotalCharges was retained only for EDA analysis(if necessary) but will be excluded from modeling to prevent leakage and multicollinearity

2.4 Outlier Handling¶

In [15]:
# We apply outlier treatment only on continuous monetary and tenure variables
outlier_cols = ['tenure', 'MonthlyCharges']

for col in outlier_cols:
    Q1 = data[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = data[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1

    lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    # Count outliers before treatment
    outliers_before = ((data[col] < lower_bound) | (data[col] > upper_bound)).sum()

    # Winsorization: cap instead of delete
    data[col] = np.where(data[col] < lower_bound, lower_bound, data[col])
    data[col] = np.where(data[col] > upper_bound, upper_bound, data[col])

    outliers_after = ((data[col] < lower_bound) | (data[col] > upper_bound)).sum()

    print(f"{col}: Outliers before = {outliers_before}, after = {outliers_after}")

tenure: Outliers before = 0, after = 0
MonthlyCharges: Outliers before = 0, after = 0

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10,4))

ax[0].set_title("Tenure Distribution")

sns.boxplot(y=data['MonthlyCharges'], ax=ax[1])

the data is well behaved with respect to outliers, this is a positive validation on the quality of the dataset.

2.5 Data Type Conversion¶

converting columns to bool¶

In [17]:
def yes_no_to_bool(series):
    return series.str.strip().str.lower().map({'yes': True, 'no': False}).astype('bool')

# Apply to all Yes/No binary columns
binary_cols = ['Partner', 'Dependents', 'PhoneService', 'PaperlessBilling', 'Churn']
for col in binary_cols:
    data[col] = yes_no_to_bool(data[col])
In [18]:
# Convert SeniorCitizen column (0/1) to boolean
data['SeniorCitizen'] = data['SeniorCitizen'].map({0: False, 1: True})

Redundant categories are being created due to upper/lower case differences for the same words. Lets make all categorical type columns have lower case strings.

2.7 Class Imbalance¶

In [26]:
data['Churn'].value_counts(normalize=True)
Out[26]:
proportion
Churn
False 0.721327
True 0.278673

Churn distribution shows moderate class imbalance. Given the imbalance is inline with what a real world situation looks like, no resampling techniques were applied at the baseline stage. Instead we will use AUC-PR as our primary metric to measure model performance so that a correct classification of a churn risk customers(Churn = True) is prioritised. Recall/ROC-AUC will be additional metrics to be considered.

3. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

helper function

In [27]:
def add_bar_labels(ax, fmt="{:.2f}"):
    for p in ax.patches:
        height = p.get_height()
        if not np.isnan(height):
            ax.annotate(fmt.format(height),
                        (p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2., height),
                        ha='center', va='bottom',
                        fontsize=9, xytext=(0, 3),
                        textcoords='offset points')

3.1 Churn Distribution¶

In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
ax = sns.countplot(x='Churn', data=data)

# Total count for percentage calculation
total = len(data)

# Add labels on top of bars
for p in ax.patches:
    count = int(p.get_height())
    percentage = 100 * count / total
    ax.text(
        p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2,
        p.get_height() + 5,
        f'{count}\n({percentage:.1f}%)',
        ha='center',
        fontsize=10
    )

plt.title('Churn Distribution')
plt.xlabel('Churn')
plt.ylabel('Number of Customers')
plt.show()
In [29]:
sns.countplot(x='Churn', data=data)
plt.title('Churn Distribution')

3.2 Univariate Analysis¶

Tenure Distribution¶

In [30]:
sns.histplot(data['tenure'], bins=30)
plt.title('Tenure Distribution')
plt.show()

Observation:
The tenure distribution is right-skewed, indicating a large proportion of relatively new customers. This suggests a need to start immediate pro-active measures to retain these early customers who are a high risk group.

MonthlyCharges Distribution¶

In [31]:
sns.histplot(data['MonthlyCharges'], bins=30)
plt.title('Monthly Charges Distribution')
plt.show()

Observation:
Monthly charges show a wide spread, indicating heterogeneous pricing plans although the most famous plans are around 20 dollars where there is a significant peak. Higher charges may increase churn risk if perceived value is low.

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6))

    data['Contract'],
    data['InternetService'],
    values=data['Churn'],
    aggfunc='mean'
)

churn_matrix.plot(kind='bar', ax=ax)

plt.title("Churn Rate by InternetService × Contract")
plt.ylabel("Churn Rate")

# Add value labels on bars
for container in ax.containers:
    for bar in container:
        height = bar.get_height()
        if not np.isnan(height):
            ax.text(
                bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
                height + 0.01,
                f"{height:.2f}",
                ha='center',
                fontsize=9
            )

plt.ylim(0, churn_matrix.max().max() + 0.1)
plt.show()

plt.show()

Newer Customers tend to go for month to month contracts, and new customers are a significant churn risk

3.5 Correlation Heatmap (Modeling Variables Only)¶

In [41]:
# Make a copy
df_num = data.copy()

# Convert bool → int for correlation
bool_cols = df_num.select_dtypes(include='bool').columns
df_num[bool_cols] = df_num[bool_cols].astype(int)

# Select ONLY numeric columns
df_num = df_num.select_dtypes(include=[np.number])

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.heatmap(
    df_num.corr(),
    annot=True,
    cmap='coolwarm',
    center=0,
    fmt=".2f"
)
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap (Numeric + Boolean Features Only)", fontsize=14)
plt.show()
In [42]:
# Select only categorical columns (excluding 'Churn' initially to handle it separately)
df_cat_corr = data.select_dtypes(include='category').copy()

# Convert categorical columns to numerical codes
for col in df_cat_corr.columns:
    df_cat_corr[col] = df_cat_corr[col].astype('category').cat.codes

# Add the 'Churn' column (which is already boolean and can be converted to int)
df_cat_corr['Churn'] = data['Churn'].astype(int)

# Calculate the correlation matrix
correlation_matrix_cat = df_cat_corr.corr(method='pearson')

# Plot the correlation heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 9))
sns.heatmap(
    correlation_matrix_cat,
    annot=True,
    cmap='coolwarm',
    center=0,
    fmt=".2f"
)
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap (Categorical Features + Churn)", fontsize=14)
plt.show()

3.6 Key EDA Insights¶

  • Churn is heavily concentrated within the first year of tenure, highlighting onboarding risk.
  • Month-to-month contracts exhibit significantly higher churn than long-term contracts.
  • Customers with higher monthly charges are more likely to churn, indicating pricing sensitivity.
  • Electronic check payment method is associated with higher churn.
  • Value-added services such as OnlineSecurity and TechSupport reduce churn probability.
  • Fiber optic customers churn more frequently than DSL users.

4. Feature Selection & Data Leakage Handling¶

Feature Selection Decision¶

  • Churn is our target variable
  • TotalCharges was excluded from modeling due to cumulative billing noise and high correlation with tenure. Only independent customer attributes were used.

Data Leakage Prevention¶

  • Cumulative billing variables such as TotalCharges were excluded.
  • All preprocessing steps except exploratory analysis were applied after train–test split.
  • Target variable was not used in any feature engineering steps.

Why TotalCharges was excluded from modeling

TotalCharges is a cumulative billing variable strongly correlated with tenure and partially driven by non-recurring charges. Including it would introduce multicollinearity and potential data leakage. Therefore, it was retained only for exploratory analysis and excluded from predictive modeling.

5. Baseline Model – Logistic Regression¶

5.1 Train–Test Split¶

In [43]:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X = data.drop(columns=['Churn','TotalCharges'])
y = data['Churn']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.3, stratify=y, random_state=42
)

X_train = pd.get_dummies(X_train, drop_first=True)
X_test = pd.get_dummies(X_test, drop_first=True)

X_test = X_test.reindex(columns=X_train.columns, fill_value=0)

5.2 Feature Scaling¶

In [75]:
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

scaler = StandardScaler()
num_cols = ['tenure','MonthlyCharges']

X_train[num_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X_train[num_cols])
X_test[num_cols] = scaler.transform(X_test[num_cols])

Feature scaling was applied to numeric variables to ensure coefficients in logistic regression are comparable and optimization converges efficiently.

5.3 Helper Function¶

In [45]:
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
    roc_auc_score,
    average_precision_score,
    precision_recall_curve
)

import numpy as np

def evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test, model_name):
    y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    precision, recall, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_prob)

    f1_scores = np.nan_to_num(2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall))

    best_idx = np.argmax(f1_scores)

    # Guard against extreme thresholds
    best_threshold = thresholds[min(best_idx, len(thresholds)-1)]

    # Prevent degenerate case for trees
    if best_threshold > 0.95:
        best_threshold = 0.5

    y_pred = (y_prob >= best_threshold).astype(int)

    return {
        "Model": model_name,
        "Best Threshold": round(best_threshold, 3),
        "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "Precision": precision_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "Recall": recall_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "F1": f1_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "AUC-PR": average_precision_score(y_test, y_prob),
        "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)
    }

5.4 Model Training¶

In [46]:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
import time


start = time.time()
lr = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, class_weight='balanced')
lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
lr_time = round(time.time() - start, 3)

lr_metrics = evaluate_model(lr, X_test, y_test, "Logistic Regression")
lr_metrics["Train_Time_sec"] = lr_time

5.5 Model Evaluation¶

Metric Rationale¶

Metric of Choice: AUC-PR and secondary as Recall and ROC-AUC

AUC-PR (Area Under the Precision–Recall Curve) was included as a key evaluation metric. AUC-PR is particularly well-suited for churn prediction problems where class imbalance exists, as it focuses on the model’s ability to correctly identify churners without being influenced by the large number of non-churn customers.

Unlike ROC-AUC, which can appear overly optimistic in imbalanced datasets, AUC-PR provides a more realistic assessment of model performance by jointly evaluating precision and recall across different decision thresholds. This makes it especially valuable for assessing the quality of churn risk ranking and supporting business decisions related to targeted retention campaigns.

Recall was selected as the secondary metric because the business cost of failing to identify a churn-prone customer is higher than the cost of incorrectly targeting a retained customer.

In [47]:
y_prob = lr.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
precision, recall, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_prob)

plt.plot(recall, precision)
plt.xlabel("Recall")
plt.ylabel("Precision")
plt.title("Precision–Recall Curve")
plt.show()

6. Advanced Models¶

We will add these 5 Models:

  1. Decision Tree

  2. Random Forest

  3. Gradient Boosting

  4. AdaBoost

  5. XGBoost

In [48]:
import time

def train_and_time(model, X_train, y_train):
    start = time.time()
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    end = time.time()
    return model, round(end - start, 3)

6.1 Decision Tree Classifier¶

In [49]:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

dt = DecisionTreeClassifier(
    max_depth=6,
    class_weight='balanced',
    random_state=42
)

start = time.time()
dt.fit(X_train, y_train)
dt_time = round(time.time() - start, 3)

dt_metrics = evaluate_model(dt, X_test, y_test, "Decision Tree")
dt_metrics["Train_Time_sec"] = dt_time

/tmp/ipython-input-2527374229.py:18: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in divide

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

for p in ax.patches:
    ax.annotate(f"{p.get_width():.3f}",
                (p.get_width(), p.get_y() + p.get_height()/2),
                va='center', ha='left', fontsize=9)

plt.title("Model Comparison Based on AUC-PR")
plt.xlabel("AUC-PR")
plt.ylabel("Model")
plt.show()

Models were compared using AUC-PR as the primary metric due to class imbalance and the business objective of correctly identifying churners. Models were ranked based on AUC-PR scores, and the top-performing model was selected for further tuning and final evaluation.

7.Model Tuning¶

Why hyperparameter tuning is required¶

Initial model results indicate that while several models demonstrate reasonable predictive performance, their default or manually chosen hyperparameters may not be optimal for maximizing the primary evaluation metric (AUC-PR). Since churn prediction involves class imbalance and asymmetric business costs, improving the ranking and identification of churners is more valuable than marginal gains in overall accuracy. Hyperparameter tuning is therefore applied to models that are both expressive enough to capture non-linear relationships and stable enough to benefit from controlled optimization.

Models Selected for Hyperparameter Tuning¶

1.Random Forest¶

2.Gradient Boosting¶

3. XGBoost¶

How the Tuning Strategy Was Decided¶

Metric-driven tuning approach¶

Hyperparameter tuning was explicitly aligned with the metric of choice: AUC-PR. Unlike accuracy or ROC-AUC, AUC-PR directly reflects the model’s ability to correctly rank churners higher than non-churners in an imbalanced dataset.

Therefore:

Scoring metric: average_precision

Objective: Improve churner ranking quality rather than raw classification accuracy

Search strategy¶

GridSearchCV (or RandomizedSearchCV for larger parameter spaces) was used to systematically explore combinations of hyperparameters that affect:

Model complexity

Generalization ability

Recall–precision balance

Cross-validation ensured that improvements were robust and not driven by a single data split.

7.1 Tuning Utility Function¶

In [56]:
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV, GridSearchCV, StratifiedKFold

def tune_model(model, param_space, X_train, y_train, model_name,
               strategy="random", n_iter=80, cv=5):

    print(f"\n🔧 Tuning {model_name}...")

    # Automatically handle class imbalance where applicable
    if 'class_weight' in model.get_params() and model_name not in ["Gradient Boosting", "XGBoost"]:
        model.set_params(class_weight="balanced")

    cv_strategy = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=cv, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

    if strategy == "random":
        search = RandomizedSearchCV(
            model, param_space, n_iter=n_iter,
            scoring="average_precision", cv=cv_strategy,
            n_jobs=-1, verbose=1, random_state=42
        )

    elif strategy == "grid":
        search = GridSearchCV(
            model, param_space,
            scoring="average_precision", cv=cv_strategy,
            n_jobs=-1, verbose=1
        )
    else:
        raise ValueError("strategy must be 'random' or 'grid'")

    search.fit(X_train, y_train)

    print(f"Best Parameters for {model_name}:")
    for k, v in search.best_params_.items():
        print(f"   {k}: {v}")

    print(f"Best CV AUC-PR: {search.best_score_:.4f}")

    return search.best_estimator_

7.2 Random forest¶

In [57]:
rf_param_space = {
    "n_estimators": [300, 500, 800],
    "max_depth": [10, 20, 30, None],
    "min_samples_split": [2, 5, 10, 20],
    "min_samples_leaf": [1, 2, 4, 10],
    "max_features": ["sqrt", "log2", 0.5],
    "max_samples": [0.7, 0.8, 0.9],
    "criterion": ["gini", "entropy"]
}

start = time.time()
rf_tuned = tune_model(
    RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42),
    rf_param_space, X_train, y_train,
    "Random Forest", strategy="random"
)
rf_time = round(time.time() - start, 3)

rf_tuned_metrics = evaluate_model(rf_tuned, X_test, y_test, "Random Forest (Tuned)")
rf_tuned_metrics["Train_Time_sec"] = rf_time

🔧 Tuning Random Forest...
Fitting 5 folds for each of 80 candidates, totalling 400 fits
Best Parameters for Random Forest:
   n_estimators: 500
   min_samples_split: 2
   min_samples_leaf: 10
   max_samples: 0.9
   max_features: sqrt
   max_depth: 30
   criterion: entropy
Best CV AUC-PR: 0.6784

Since the dataset exhibits class imbalance and the business objective prioritizes the identification of churners, class weighting was applied to penalize misclassification of the minority class. Using class_weight='balanced' ensures the model learns decision boundaries that better capture churn risk, thereby improving recall and AUC-PR.

In [58]:
tuned_model_results = []
tuned_model_results.append(rf_tuned_metrics)

7.3 gradient boosting¶

In [59]:
gb = GradientBoostingClassifier(
    random_state=42,
    validation_fraction=0.1,
    n_iter_no_change=15,
    tol=1e-4
)
In [60]:
gb_param_space = {
    "n_estimators": [200, 400, 600, 800],
    "learning_rate": [0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1],
    "max_depth": [2, 3, 4, 5],
    "min_samples_leaf": [1, 3, 5, 10],
    "min_samples_split": [2, 5, 10],
    "subsample": [0.6, 0.75, 0.9, 1.0],
    "max_features": ["sqrt", "log2", None]
}


start = time.time()
gb_tuned = tune_model(
    gb,
    gb_param_space,
    X_train,
    y_train,
    "Gradient Boosting",
    strategy="random",
    n_iter=120,      # increase search coverage
    cv=5
)
gb_time = round(time.time() - start, 3)

gb_tuned_metrics = evaluate_model(gb_tuned, X_test, y_test, "Gradient Boosting (Tuned)")
gb_tuned_metrics["Train_Time_sec"] = gb_time

🔧 Tuning Gradient Boosting...
Fitting 5 folds for each of 120 candidates, totalling 600 fits

   subsample: 0.75
   n_estimators: 600
   min_samples_split: 2
   min_samples_leaf: 10
   max_features: None
   max_depth: 3

tuned_model_results.append(gb_tuned_metrics)

7.4 xgboost¶

In [62]:
from scipy.stats import uniform, randint

xgb_param_space = {
    "n_estimators": randint(300, 1500),
    "learning_rate": uniform(0.005, 0.15),
    "max_depth": randint(2, 10),
    "min_child_weight": randint(1, 12),
    "subsample": uniform(0.6, 0.4),
    "colsample_bytree": uniform(0.6, 0.4),
    "colsample_bylevel": uniform(0.6, 0.4),
    "gamma": uniform(0, 8),
    "reg_alpha": uniform(0, 3),
    "reg_lambda": uniform(0.5, 4),
    "max_delta_step": randint(0, 10)
}


start = time.time()

xgb_tuned = tune_model(
    XGBClassifier(
        eval_metric="logloss",
        tree_method="hist",
        random_state=42
    ),
    xgb_param_space,
    X_train,
    y_train,
    "XGBoost",
    strategy="random",
    n_iter=200,   # much deeper exploration
    cv=5
)

xgb_tuned.set_params(
    early_stopping_rounds=20,
    eval_metric="aucpr"
)

xgb_tuned.fit(
    X_train, y_train,
    eval_set=[(X_test, y_test)],
    verbose=False
)


xgb_time = round(time.time() - start, 3)


xgb_tuned_metrics = evaluate_model(xgb_tuned, X_test, y_test, "XGBoost (Tuned)")
xgb_tuned_metrics["Train_Time_sec"] = xgb_time

🔧 Tuning XGBoost...
Fitting 5 folds for each of 200 candidates, totalling 1000 fits
Best Parameters for XGBoost:
   colsample_bylevel: 0.749816047538945
   colsample_bytree: 0.9802857225639665
   gamma: 5.855951534491241
   learning_rate: 0.0947987726295555
   max_delta_step: 6
   max_depth: 3
   min_child_weight: 3
   n_estimators: 630
   reg_alpha: 1.3777466758976016
   reg_lambda: 1.8348344445560874
   subsample: 0.6571467271687763
Best CV AUC-PR: 0.6838
In [63]:
tuned_model_results.append(xgb_tuned_metrics)

7.6 final comparison table with improvement¶

In [71]:
# Build baseline and tuned tables
baseline_models = pd.DataFrame([rf_metrics, gb_metrics, xgb_metrics])
tuned_models = pd.DataFrame([rf_tuned_metrics, gb_tuned_metrics, xgb_tuned_metrics])

# Create consistent merge keys
baseline_models['Model_Key'] = baseline_models['Model'].str.lower().str.strip()
tuned_models['Model_Key'] = tuned_models['Model'].str.lower().str.replace(' (tuned)', '', regex=False).str.strip()

# Merge
comparison = pd.merge(
    baseline_models,
    tuned_models,
    on='Model_Key',
    suffixes=('_Untuned', '_Tuned')
)

# Compute improvements
# Compute improvements
for metric in ['AUC-PR', 'Recall', 'ROC-AUC']:
    comparison[f'{metric}_Improvement'] = comparison[f'{metric}_Tuned'] - comparison[f'{metric}_Untuned']
    comparison[f'{metric}_Improvement_%'] = (
        comparison[f'{metric}_Improvement'] / comparison[f'{metric}_Untuned'] * 100
    )


# Final executive table
final_comparison_table = comparison[
    [
        'Model_Untuned',

        'AUC-PR_Untuned', 'AUC-PR_Tuned', 'AUC-PR_Improvement_%',
        'Recall_Untuned', 'Recall_Tuned', 'Recall_Improvement_%',
        'ROC-AUC_Untuned', 'ROC-AUC_Tuned',
        'ROC-AUC_Improvement_%'
    ]
].round(3)

final_comparison_table
Out[71]:
Model_Untuned AUC-PR_Untuned AUC-PR_Tuned AUC-PR_Improvement_% Recall_Untuned Recall_Tuned Recall_Improvement_% ROC-AUC_Untuned ROC-AUC_Tuned ROC-AUC_Improvement_%
0 Random Forest 0.678 0.682 0.571 0.816 0.738 -9.598 0.853 0.854 0.169
1 Gradient Boosting 0.686 0.685 -0.140 0.789 0.776 -1.743 0.855 0.855 0.012
2 XGBoost 0.684 0.686 0.391 0.784 0.743 -5.263 0.853 0.856 0.298

7.7 Create Clean Untuned vs Tuned Time Comparison Table¶

In [72]:
time_comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": ["Random Forest", "Gradient Boosting", "XGBoost"],

    "Untuned_Time_sec": [
        rf_metrics["Train_Time_sec"],
        gb_metrics["Train_Time_sec"],
        xgb_metrics["Train_Time_sec"]
    ],

    "Tuned_Time_sec": [
        rf_tuned_metrics["Train_Time_sec"],
        gb_tuned_metrics["Train_Time_sec"],
        xgb_tuned_metrics["Train_Time_sec"]
    ],

    "Untuned_AUC_PR": [
        rf_metrics["AUC-PR"],
        gb_metrics["AUC-PR"],
        xgb_metrics["AUC-PR"]
    ],

    "Tuned_AUC_PR": [
        rf_tuned_metrics["AUC-PR"],
        gb_tuned_metrics["AUC-PR"],
        xgb_tuned_metrics["AUC-PR"]
    ]
})

# Compute improvements
time_comparison["Time_Increase_%"] = (
    (time_comparison["Tuned_Time_sec"] - time_comparison["Untuned_Time_sec"]) /
     time_comparison["Untuned_Time_sec"] * 100
)

time_comparison["AUC_PR_Improvement"] = (
    time_comparison["Tuned_AUC_PR"] - time_comparison["Untuned_AUC_PR"]
)

time_comparison["AUC_PR_Improvement_%"] = (
    time_comparison["AUC_PR_Improvement"] /
    time_comparison["Untuned_AUC_PR"] * 100
)

time_comparison.round(3)
Out[72]:
Model Untuned_Time_sec Tuned_Time_sec Untuned_AUC_PR Tuned_AUC_PR Time_Increase_% AUC_PR_Improvement AUC_PR_Improvement_%
0 Random Forest 2.451 844.213 0.678 0.682 34343.615 0.004 0.571
1 Gradient Boosting 3.597 564.846 0.686 0.685 15603.253 -0.001 -0.140
2 XGBoost 0.221 349.059 0.684 0.686 157845.249 0.003 0.391
